mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the division of geological time into four great eons (Hadean, Archean, Proterozoic, Phanerozoic) and the development of the geological timescale spanning all eras.
Britain's geological formation from Precambrian through Carboniferous coal measures, Mesozoic chalk and limestone, to Quaternary ice ages spans all major geological eras and periods.
Directly covers the Cambrian Period (541-485 Mya) and the Cambrian Explosion, the rapid diversification of complex animal life that defines the start of the Paleozoic Era.
The Ediacara biota from the late Precambrian (635-541 Mya) represent the key multicellular life forms of Precambrian time, immediately preceding the Cambrian explosion.
The Late Devonian Extinction (372-359 Mya) occurred during the Devonian Period of the Paleozoic Era, devastating marine ecosystems and shaping the trajectory of vertebrate evolution.
The Permian-Triassic extinction (252 Mya) marks the boundary between the Permian (last Paleozoic period) and Triassic (first Mesozoic period), the most severe mass extinction in Earth's history.
The K-T boundary at 66 Mya marks the end of the Cretaceous Period (last Mesozoic) and the start of the Tertiary Period (Cenozoic), with the asteroid impact eliminating non-avian dinosaurs.
The PETM occurred approximately 56 Mya during the early Tertiary Period of the Cenozoic Era, a major global warming event that drove mammalian diversification.
Mammalian evolution covers the Mesozoic origins of early mammals through their Cenozoic adaptive radiation, including Pleistocene megafaunal extinctions, spanning multiple geological periods.
Pleistocene glaciations and earlier Snowball Earth events relate directly to the Quaternary Period and the Pleistocene Epoch, as well as Precambrian glacial episodes.
Earth's formation approximately 4.5 billion years ago and earliest evidence of life address the beginning of Precambrian time and the formation of the oldest rocks.
Early geology covers the identification and dating of geological strata, the development of the geological timescale through the work of Smith, Lyell, and Cuvier across all major eras.
Crocodilian evolution from diverse Triassic archosaurs through Cretaceous survival spans the Mesozoic Era, illustrating evolutionary changes across Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods.
Horse evolution from Eohippus approximately 55 Mya to modern Equus spans the Tertiary Period of the Cenozoic Era.
Whale evolution from land-dwelling mammals to fully aquatic forms over approximately 50 million years documents a major Tertiary Period evolutionary transformation.
The fish-tetrapod transition occurred in the Devonian Period, with key fossils like Tiktaalik documenting this evolutionary milestone of the Paleozoic Era.
